_meta.comment: Single source of truth for cross-device persistent metrics. Pages fetch this on load. Updates flow: Raquel tells Claude in chat -> Claude commits update -> live on every device.

ideas.title: I can't function if I'm not making something
ideas.note:
Thesis: Creating isn't a hobby for her — it's regulation. When she stops making, the upset has nowhere to go and everything gets louder. The video probes the WHY: what changes between 'consuming' and 'creating,' between writing in a journal and performing for a camera.

Angles (pick one or stack):
1) Consume vs create — scrolling for hours makes everything heavier; making one mediocre video makes it lift. Why?
2) Perform vs journal — writing it down doesn't move it. She specifically needs the camera, the take, the watching it back. The performance is the load-bearing part.
3) The outlet question — what does an unprocessed feeling actually do when it has nowhere to land? Apparently it just sits on you until you put it somewhere.

Hook lines:
· I figured out why I was miserable for months. It's embarrassing.
· Apparently I have to be performing or I fall apart. Cool, normal discovery to make at 23.
· I can't function if I'm not making something. Let me explain why.
· Stuck and upset for months. Started filming again. Mood: completely different. We're going to figure out why on camera.

Why it hooks wide: 'I'm only okay when I'm making something' is a feeling a lot of people half-have but can't name — taps the consume-vs-create exhaustion without sounding like a productivity sermon.

ideas.title: The reason every actor you love ends up in rehab
ideas.note:
Thesis: The wiring that makes you good at the craft — high sensitivity, deep empathy, the ability to feel your way into another person's interior — is the exact wiring that gets shredded by being perceived. Fame doesn't break artists at random. It targets the asset. The 'tortured artist' cliché isn't a personality flaw, it's a side effect. Self-aware angle: she's trying to become famous, watching how the people before her broke, taking notes so she doesn't repeat them.

Pillar fit: Primary Pillar 2 · Acting / Visibility / Becoming Known (the becoming-famous lens, the watching-everyone-before-her angle). Strong secondary Pillar 4 · ND Chaos / Functional Burnout (nervous-system wiring, infrastructure for sensitivity). Cross-pillar — flag for use as a tentpole / pinned video, not a quick TOFU throwaway.

Angles (pick one or stack):
1) The cost framing — empathy is an occupational hazard. Here's how the bill comes due. The same nervous system that lets you cry on cue is the one that can't stop reading the room when 4M people are in it.
2) The infrastructure framing — what does an empath who's about to get more visible have to build to survive it? Boundaries that aren't walls. Off-stage people who aren't fans. Processing practices that aren't 'self-care.' The infrastructure question nobody answers because everyone's busy being broken on camera.
3) The personal-stakes framing — 'I'm trying to become famous and I'm watching everyone before me. Taking notes on how they broke. Not as a warning. As a study guide.'

Hook lines:
· Matthew Perry. Brad and Angelina. Half the actors I love. Their lives went sideways and it wasn't a coincidence.
· I'm trying to become famous so I've been taking notes on how everyone before me broke.
· The wiring that makes you good at the work is the same wiring fame chews to pieces. I don't think you get to keep one without the other.
· Empathy is the asset. It's also the injury. The job is figuring out how to have both without ending up in rehab.

Why it hooks wide: Names real public figures people have opinions about, then turns the cliché into a thesis. 'Tortured artist' is a phrase everyone has heard and nobody has examined. The 'I'm taking notes on the people who broke before me' frame is novel — it's not the usual 'fame is bad' takedown, it's a working person studying her field. Sarah-coded: she's the woman who shelved the creative thing partly because she clocked the cost. This says the cost out loud.

FLAG: Sensitive material — addiction, mental health adjacent. Treat references to real public figures as factual (their public struggles are public record), not as gossip. Don't punch down. Don't list anyone whose primary public association is suicide. The frame is 'study,' not 'cautionary tale.' Raquel said she 'didn't fully land it' — script should keep the curiosity tone, not pretend to a conclusion she hasn't reached. End on the open question (what does the infrastructure look like) not on a tidy answer.
